For a truncate-and-reload pattern, Dynamic Tables handle this via full recompute on the next refresh cycle.

options.text: Dynamic Tables cannot handle TRUNCATE + INSERT patterns; use a Materialized View which detects truncate operations and triggers a full refresh
options.explanation: Dynamic Tables handle truncate patterns by detecting the change and doing a full recompute refresh.

questions.stem: A company wants to ensure that all PII columns across all tables in Snowflake are identified and catalogued. A security engineer recommends using Snowflake's built-in classification feature. What does Snowflake Data Classification do?

options.text: Data Classification automatically applies masking policies to all columns it identifies as PII
options.explanation: Classification only tags columns; applying masking policies requires a separate step (tag-based masking policy or direct policy assignment).

options.text: Data Classification analyses column names and sample data values to assign system-defined tags such as SEMANTIC_CATEGORY (e.g., NAME, EMAIL, SSN) and PRIVACY_CATEGORY (e.g., IDENTIFIER, QUASI_IDENTIFIER) to columns
options.explanation: Snowflake Data Classification is an automated ML-based feature that analyses column names and a sample of values to detect PII and other sensitive data categories. It assigns system-defined tags: SEMANTIC_CATEGORY captures the data type meaning (NAME, EMAIL_ADDRESS, NATIONAL_IDENTIFIER, etc.) and PRIVACY_CATEGORY captures the sensitivity level (IDENTIFIER, QUASI_IDENTIFIER, SENSITIVE). These tags are then available for governance queries, tag-based masking policies, and compliance reporting.

options.text: Data Classification is a manual process where administrators tag each column using a classification wizard in Snowsight
options.explanation: Classification is an automated ML-driven process, not a manual wizard.

options.text: Data Classification integrates with GDPR and HIPAA compliance frameworks and automatically generates required audit reports
options.explanation: Data Classification identifies sensitive data but does not generate compliance reports or integrate directly with GDPR/HIPAA frameworks.

questions.stem: A company has a masking policy that returns CURRENT_ROLE() in its logic. An analyst uses the ANALYST role and runs: USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL; SELECT credit_card FROM customers. The analyst's secondary roles include FINANCE_TEAM which should see unmasked data per the policy. Will the masking policy reveal unmasked data to this analyst?

options.text: No; CURRENT_ROLE() only returns the primary active role (ANALYST), so the masking policy returns masked data even though FINANCE_TEAM is a secondary role
options.explanation: is correct and represents an important security subtlety. CURRENT_ROLE() always returns the current PRIMARY role — the role that was explicitly activated with USE ROLE. Secondary roles (activated via USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL) provide additional privileges for data access but are NOT reflected by CURRENT_ROLE(). A masking policy using CURRENT_ROLE() would evaluate to 'ANALYST' in this session, not 'FINANCE_TEAM'. This is why best practice dictates using IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FINANCE_TEAM') in masking policies instead of CURRENT_ROLE() — IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() returns TRUE if the role is active anywhere in the session (primary or secondary).

options.text: Yes; USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL activates all secondary roles, and CURRENT_ROLE() reflects all active roles during masking policy evaluation
options.explanation: CURRENT_ROLE() is not affected by USE SECONDARY ROLES.

options.text: Yes; masking policies always evaluate ALL roles in the role hierarchy regardless of what CURRENT_ROLE() returns
options.explanation: Masking policies evaluate the context provided by the specific function used.

options.text: No; USE SECONDARY ROLES requires a special parameter MASKING_POLICY_INCLUDE_SECONDARY_ROLES = TRUE to affect masking policy evaluation
options.explanation: No such parameter exists.

questions.stem: A governance team needs to implement object-level access control so that a given user can see rows in the ORDERS table only for customers in their assigned territory, with the territory list stored in a separate ACCESS_MAP table. Select TWO statements that accurately describe Row Access Policy implementation for this requirement. (Select TWO)

options.text: A Row Access Policy returns a BOOLEAN expression evaluated per row; rows where the expression returns TRUE are visible to the querying user
options.explanation: This is the fundamental mechanism: a Row Access Policy is a function that returns a BOOLEAN value for each row. Snowflake evaluates this function as an implicit WHERE clause — TRUE means the row is returned, FALSE means it is filtered out.

options.text: Row Access Policies can only filter on columns present in the table they are applied to; they cannot perform subqueries against external mapping tables
options.explanation: Row Access Policies can reference external tables via subqueries.

options.text: A Row Access Policy can reference external mapping tables via a subquery: RETURNS BOOLEAN -> territory_col IN (SELECT territory FROM ACCESS_MAP WHERE user = CURRENT_USER())
options.explanation: Row Access Policies explicitly support subqueries against mapping tables as part of the policy body. This is the recommended pattern for dynamic, data-driven access control — the policy references CURRENT_USER() (or CURRENT_ROLE()) and looks up allowed values in a mapping table. The subquery is evaluated per session, not per row, for performance.

options.text: Row Access Policies must be applied to the primary key column of the target table
options.explanation: Row Access Policies are applied to a column that represents the filtering dimension (like territory), not necessarily the primary key.

options.text: Multiple Row Access Policies can be applied to the same table simultaneously; Snowflake evaluates all policies and returns the union of visible rows
options.explanation: Snowflake allows at most one Row Access Policy per table; applying a second policy requires first removing the existing one.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account administrator wants to understand which users currently have direct grants of a specific privilege vs those who inherit it through role hierarchy. Which approach provides this analysis?

options.text: Query ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS to see all effective privileges for each user including inherited ones
options.explanation: GRANTS_TO_USERS shows which roles are granted to users, not which privileges; it does not flatten effective privilege sets.

options.text: Query ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES to find roles with the privilege, then trace role grants from ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES (for role-to-role grants) and ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_USERS to identify which users have those roles
options.explanation: Snowflake's privilege model grants privileges to roles (not directly to users), and users get privileges by being granted roles. To understand who has a specific privilege: (1) query ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES to find all roles that have the privilege; (2) follow the role hierarchy (roles granted to other roles in GRANTS_TO_ROLES where the grantee_type = 'ROLE'); (3) query GRANTS_TO_USERS to see which users have any of those roles. This multi-step graph traversal reflects Snowflake's RBAC model.

options.text: Run SHOW GRANTS TO USER username; this returns all effective privileges including those inherited through role hierarchies
options.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO USER shows roles granted to the user, not effective privileges through the hierarchy.

options.text: Run SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE tablename to see all users with access to the table including through role inheritance
options.explanation: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE shows roles that have access, not users directly.

questions.stem: A development team accidentally dropped a table containing 6 months of financial records. The table had DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. The drop was discovered 10 days after the event. Can the table be recovered, and if so, how?

options.text: Yes; use UNDROP TABLE table_name since Snowflake retains dropped objects in Fail-Safe for 7 additional days beyond Time Travel
options.explanation: UNDROP uses Time Travel, not Fail-Safe, so it will fail after 7 days.

options.text: No; the 7-day Time Travel window has expired; Time Travel recovery is no longer possible. The data may still exist in Fail-Safe but recovery requires contacting Snowflake Support
options.explanation: is correct. The table had 7-day Time Travel (DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7), and the drop occurred 10 days ago — the Time Travel window has expired. UNDROP TABLE (which uses Time Travel) will fail. However, Snowflake's Fail-Safe provides an additional 7-day window after Time Travel expires, managed exclusively by Snowflake Support. Since 10 days have passed and Time Travel is 7 days, Fail-Safe would have started 3 days ago and runs until 14 days post-drop. The data may still be in Fail-Safe — the customer should contact Snowflake Support immediately, as the Fail-Safe window may still be open.

options.text: Yes; run CREATE TABLE table_name CLONE table_name AT(OFFSET => -864000) to clone from 10 days ago
options.explanation: You cannot clone a dropped table.

options.text: No; once a table is dropped, all data is immediately deleted from Snowflake's storage for security
options.explanation: Data is not immediately deleted on DROP; Time Travel and Fail-Safe retain it.

questions.stem: A company wants to implement column-level security where the same employee ID column is masked differently depending on whether the querying role is HR, FINANCE, or AUDIT: HR sees full IDs, FINANCE sees partially masked IDs (last 4 digits only), and AUDIT sees fully masked (all X's). Which masking policy construct correctly implements this three-tier logic?

options.text: Create three separate masking policies and apply all three to the EMPLOYEE_ID column simultaneously; Snowflake selects the appropriate policy based on CURRENT_ROLE()

Create a single masking policy with a CASE expression using IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() to return the appropriate value for each role: CASE WHEN IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('HR') THEN val WHEN IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('FINANCE') THEN ... ELSE 'XXXX' END
options.explanation: A single masking policy with a CASE expression is the idiomatic Snowflake approach for multi-tier column masking. Using IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION() (rather than CURRENT_ROLE()) ensures that the check works correctly even when the querying role inherits permissions through secondary roles or role hierarchy. The CASE expression evaluates conditions in order: if HR role is active, return the unmasked value; if FINANCE role is active, return partial masking; otherwise (including AUDIT or any other role), return fully masked. Only one masking policy can be applied to a column at a time, so A (three separate policies) is invalid. C (three secure views) works but loses the policy-governance benefit — anyone with direct table access bypasses the views.

options.text: Use three different secure views — one per role — each with its own masking logic, and grant each role SELECT on their respective view
Create a UDF that takes CURRENT_ROLE() as input and returns the appropriate masked value; apply the UDF as a computed column on the table
options.explanation: is a valid approach but more complex than the native masking policy; also, computed columns behave differently from masking policies.

questions.stem: A company runs a Snowflake stored procedure with EXECUTE AS OWNER. A security review flags this as a potential privilege escalation risk. Which statement correctly explains the risk and how EXECUTE AS CALLER mitigates it?

options.text: EXECUTE AS OWNER means the procedure runs with the privileges of whoever owns the procedure (e.g., SYSADMIN). A caller with fewer privileges can perform actions (like accessing tables they don't have privileges on) by invoking the procedure. EXECUTE AS CALLER restricts the procedure to only the caller's own privileges
options.explanation: is correct and describes the fundamental stored procedure privilege model. EXECUTE AS OWNER (the default) causes the procedure to execute with the owner role's privileges — if the owner is SYSADMIN and has SELECT on sensitive tables, any user who can CALL the procedure effectively gets those privileges for the duration of the procedure. This is intentional for controlled privilege elevation but is a risk if not carefully designed. EXECUTE AS CALLER runs the procedure with the caller's own privileges — the procedure can only access what the caller themselves can access, eliminating the privilege elevation vector. This is important for stored procedures that should not grant temporary elevated access.

options.text: EXECUTE AS OWNER prevents the procedure from accessing any data; EXECUTE AS CALLER allows the procedure to access data in the caller's current schema only
options.explanation: EXECUTE AS OWNER grants the owner's privileges, not zero access.

options.text: The risk only applies to JavaScript stored procedures; Python and Java stored procedures always execute as CALLER
options.explanation: This applies to all stored procedure language types.

options.text: EXECUTE AS OWNER and EXECUTE AS CALLER are equivalent in terms of data access; the difference is only in audit logging
options.explanation: The difference is substantive, not just in audit logs.

questions.stem: A company's SYSADMIN creates a new database PROD_DB and grants USAGE to ANALYST_ROLE. However, ANALYST_ROLE users report they cannot see any schemas or tables inside PROD_DB when they run SHOW SCHEMAS IN DATABASE PROD_DB. Which grant is missing?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON ALL SCHEMAS IN DATABASE PROD_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE
options.explanation: The Snowflake privilege model requires USAGE to be granted at every level of the object hierarchy: USAGE on the database allows navigating into the database, but USAGE on each schema is also required to see objects within the schema. Without GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA (or USAGE ON ALL SCHEMAS IN DATABASE), users can connect to the database but cannot see schemas or their contents. This is a common misconfiguration. GRANT FUTURE USAGE ON SCHEMAS IN DATABASE is also needed if new schemas will be created later.

options.text: GRANT REFERENCES ON DATABASE PROD_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE
options.explanation: REFERENCES is used for column-level referencing, not schema navigation.

options.text: GRANT MONITOR ON DATABASE PROD_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE
options.explanation: MONITOR provides operational monitoring privileges, not access to see schemas.

options.text: GRANT ALL ON DATABASE PROD_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE
options.explanation: is overly permissive and grants all privileges including ownership-level actions.

questions.stem: An architect is evaluating whether to use a Materialized View or a standard view for a dashboard that runs aggregations over a 500 million row base table. The base table receives approximately 10,000 row inserts per hour via Snowpipe. Which scenario makes the Materialized View the WRONG choice?

options.text: The dashboard query includes a complex PIVOT operation that the Materialized View engine does not support
options.explanation: Snowflake Materialized Views have significant SQL restrictions — they cannot include PIVOT, UNPIVOT, certain window functions, UDFs, recursive CTEs, or other complex SQL constructs. If the dashboard query requires a PIVOT operation, a Materialized View simply cannot be used. The architect must use a standard view (with full-table scan each time) or a Dynamic Table which supports arbitrary SQL. B describes a good Materialized View use case: simple aggregation, low DML rate. C might create a freshness problem (MV refresh lag vs 1-minute freshness requirement) but is a trade-off consideration, not an absolute disqualifier. D describes exactly why MVs are beneficial — the small result relative to the large base table makes pre-computation cost-effective.

options.text: The dashboard query is simple (SELECT region, SUM(revenue) FROM sales GROUP BY region) and the base table receives fewer than 1,000 updates per day
The 500 million row table grows by 10,000 rows per hour and the dashboard expects results within 1 minute of ingestion
The aggregation result set (100 rows) is much smaller than the base table, making pre-computation clearly beneficial

questions.stem: A company wants to implement Snowflake Budgets to monitor spending for a specific data engineering team that uses 5 dedicated warehouses. The ACCOUNTADMIN creates a custom budget. Which statement correctly describes what a custom Snowflake Budget can and cannot do?

options.text: A custom Budget monitors credit consumption for a specific set of objects (like the 5 warehouses) and sends email alerts when projected spend exceeds the budget threshold, but it does not suspend or throttle the warehouses
options.explanation: Snowflake Budgets are observability and alerting tools, not enforcement tools. A custom budget scoped to specific objects (warehouses, serverless services, etc.) tracks their credit consumption and projects future spend. When the projection indicates the budget will be exceeded, email alerts are sent to designated contacts. However, Budgets do not suspend warehouses, throttle compute, or take any enforcement action — they purely observe and alert. Resource Monitors remain the enforcement mechanism.

options.text: A custom Budget can suspend all 5 warehouses immediately when the budget threshold is reached, acting as a Resource Monitor replacement for object groups
options.explanation: Budgets do not suspend warehouses; only Resource Monitors can automatically suspend or notify on warehouse credit limits.

options.text: Custom Budgets can only monitor account-wide spending; per-object group scoping requires Resource Monitors
options.explanation: Custom Budgets explicitly support object-group scoping, which is their key differentiator from the account-level budget.

options.text: Custom Budgets integrate with cloud cost management tools (AWS Cost Explorer, Azure Cost Management) to aggregate Snowflake and non-Snowflake spending in a unified view
options.explanation: Budgets are Snowflake-internal and do not integrate with cloud cost management platforms such as AWS Cost Explorer or Azure Cost Management.

questions.stem: A data provider creates a share and includes a Secure Materialized View (SMV) over a 10 billion row table. The SMV pre-aggregates data to 1 million rows. Consumers query the SMV frequently. Which statement correctly describes how compute costs are allocated for consumers querying this SMV?

options.text: The provider pays all compute costs because the SMV is maintained on provider storage
options.explanation: Consumers pay for their own warehouse compute on queries.

options.text: The provider pays for the serverless compute used to refresh and maintain the SMV; consumers pay for the compute on their own warehouses used to query the pre-aggregated SMV results
options.explanation: Costs are split by function: the provider incurs the serverless compute cost of maintaining and refreshing the Secure Materialized View (keeping the pre-aggregated 1 million row result current as the 10 billion row source table changes). The consumer incurs the virtual warehouse compute cost of querying the pre-aggregated SMV — which is a query against 1 million rows rather than 10 billion, so the consumer's query cost is dramatically lower than querying the base data. This cost model means SMVs can be economically beneficial: the provider absorbs SMV maintenance to give consumers cheap, fast queries against pre-computed results.

options.text: Neither party pays for compute since SMVs are pre-computed and cached by Snowflake's infrastructure at no cost
options.explanation: SMV maintenance is not free; it consumes serverless compute.

options.text: Consumers pay for all compute including the SMV maintenance cost, billed retroactively each month
options.explanation: Consumers do not pay for SMV maintenance.

questions.stem: A company's architect needs to determine the minimum Snowflake edition required to use Failover Groups for cross-region DR. Which edition is required?

options.text: Standard edition — Failover Groups are available on all Snowflake editions
options.explanation: Standard edition does not support Failover Groups.

options.text: Enterprise edition — Failover Groups require Enterprise edition or higher
options.explanation: Failover Groups (and the underlying database replication that powers them) require Enterprise edition or higher. Standard edition accounts can use Snowflake features but do not have access to cross-account or cross-region replication capabilities needed for DR. This is an important consideration when an organization is selecting their Snowflake edition and has business continuity requirements. Replication Groups (for non-failover use cases) also require Enterprise.

options.text: Business Critical edition — the DR capabilities of Failover Groups require Business Critical for compliance
options.explanation: While Business Critical includes Failover Groups, Enterprise is the minimum required.

options.text: Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) — Failover Groups are exclusive to dedicated VPS deployments
options.explanation: VPS is a deployment model, not a prerequisite for Failover Groups.

questions.reference: [Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: A company queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE and notices that their cloud_services_credits_used exceeds 10% of their total compute credits. Snowflake charges cloud services credits when they exceed 10% of daily compute credits. Which workload type is most likely the primary driver of high cloud services credit consumption?

options.text: Long-running single analytical queries on XLarge warehouses
options.explanation: Long-running queries have low cloud services ratios because the warehouse compute credits dominate the cloud services overhead.

options.text: Thousands of very short queries (sub-second) where query compile time and metadata operations dominate total execution time
options.explanation: Cloud services layer operations include query compilation, metadata management, transaction coordination, and other non-compute activities. For long analytical queries, the compute credits (warehouse running) vastly exceed the cloud services overhead — the ratio stays below 10%. But for thousands of very short queries, each query still requires cloud services overhead (parse, plan, coordinate) but runs for only a fraction of a second on the warehouse. The cloud services overhead per query becomes a large proportion of the total, potentially exceeding the 10% free tier.

options.text: Large COPY INTO bulk loads of compressed Parquet files from S3
options.explanation: Large COPY INTO bulk loads have high compute credit consumption, which keeps the cloud services ratio below 10%.

options.text: Automatic Clustering running overnight on a 5 TB table
options.explanation: Automatic Clustering uses serverless compute which is billed separately from cloud services credits.

questions.stem: A company wants to optimize their Snowflake storage costs. An architect reviews ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and finds several tables with extremely high FAILSAFE_BYTES relative to their ACTIVE_BYTES. Select TWO actions that would reduce Fail-Safe storage costs for these tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: Set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on the tables; reducing Time Travel to 0 also reduces Fail-Safe storage because Fail-Safe only retains data for the period after Time Travel
options.explanation: Time Travel retention does NOT affect Fail-Safe; Fail-Safe is always 7 days for permanent tables regardless of DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS.

options.text: Change the tables from permanent tables to TRANSIENT tables; Transient tables have no Fail-Safe period
options.explanation: Transient tables do not have a Fail-Safe period in Snowflake — they only have Time Travel (up to 1 day) and then data is permanently deleted. Converting permanent tables to Transient tables eliminates all Fail-Safe storage overhead. This is appropriate for tables holding reproducible or non-critical data (staging tables, derived datasets) where Fail-Safe recovery is not needed.

options.text: Contact Snowflake Support and request early Fail-Safe deletion for the identified tables
options.explanation: is not a standard customer capability; customers cannot request early Fail-Safe deletion. E would work only if the move effectively changes the table type, but simply moving to a database with lower retention doesn't change Fail-Safe duration.

options.text: Reduce the number of DML operations on the tables; Fail-Safe retains micro-partitions that were modified, so fewer modifications = less Fail-Safe storage
options.explanation: Fail-Safe retains the historical micro-partitions created by DML operations — every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE creates new micro-partitions and marks old ones for Fail-Safe retention. Tables with high DML throughput accumulate large Fail-Safe footprints. Reducing unnecessary DML (e.g., eliminating full-table refreshes where incremental updates suffice) directly reduces the volume of micro-partitions entering Fail-Safe.

options.text: Move the tables to a different database with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 to inherit zero-day retention settings

questions.stem: A company's data engineering team uses a Large warehouse for a pipeline that runs 200 separate MERGE statements in sequence. Each MERGE takes 45 seconds and the warehouse is auto-suspended between MERGEs with AUTO_SUSPEND = 60. What is the total warehouse credit consumption for the 200 MERGEs if a Large warehouse costs 8 credits/hour?

options.text: 8 credits (200 × 45 seconds = 2.5 hours of runtime, but the warehouse is billed by the second)
options.explanation: Billing is per second with a 60-second minimum per resume, not by raw runtime alone; ignoring the idle time after each MERGE significantly undercounts.

options.text: Approximately 13.3 credits (200 × 45 seconds = 9,000 seconds of actual query time, but the warehouse runs for at least 60 seconds after each MERGE due to AUTO_SUSPEND = 60, so 200 × 60 seconds minimum = 12,000 seconds = 3.3 hours × 4 credits... recalculated: 200 × 60s = 200 minutes, but since the warehouse stays up for queries in the same minute it may consolidate)
options.explanation: The calculation is confused and internally inconsistent; the 200-minute figure does not account for the resume minimum billing correctly.

options.text: Approximately 26.7 credits: 200 MERGE statements each run 45 seconds, but the warehouse stays active for a full 60 seconds after each MERGE before suspending; total active time = 200 × 60 seconds = 12,000 seconds = 3.33 hours × 8 credits/hour = 26.7 credits
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake bills virtual warehouses per second with a minimum of 60 seconds per resume event. With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 and each MERGE taking only 45 seconds, the warehouse suspends after each MERGE (45s query + 60s idle = 105s cycle per MERGE). For 200 sequential MERGEs each causing a resume-run-idle-suspend cycle: each cycle bills at least 60 seconds (the minimum per resume). Using the simplified model of 60s per cycle: 200 × 60 seconds = 12,000 seconds = 3.33 hours × 8 credits/hour = 26.7 credits. The key architectural lesson is that AUTO_SUSPEND set too aggressively with frequent short queries causes excessive resume overhead. The optimal fix is to run all 200 MERGEs in a single session without intermediate suspension.

options.text: 8 credits exactly, because Snowflake bills virtual warehouses in 1-credit minimum increments regardless of duration
options.explanation: Snowflake does not bill in 1-credit minimum increments; it bills per second (with a 60-second minimum per resume event, not per total session).

questions.stem: A company ingests data from 10,000 small JSON files (average 50KB each) every hour into a Snowflake table using COPY INTO. The load consistently takes 90 minutes despite the data volume being only 500MB total. What is the most likely cause and remedy?

options.text: The files are too large for the Medium warehouse; increase to XLarge to handle more data per node
options.explanation: The bottleneck is file count overhead, not data volume per warehouse node; a larger warehouse cannot fix per-file enumeration overhead.

options.text: The files are too small — 10,000 files at 50KB each causes excessive file enumeration and per-file overhead in COPY INTO; consolidate files into fewer, larger files (100-250MB each) before loading
options.explanation: Snowflake's COPY INTO is optimised for files in the 100MB–250MB range. Very small files (50KB) create significant overhead: for each file, Snowflake must enumerate it in the stage, open it, parse metadata, validate format, and process the load — this overhead is roughly constant per file regardless of size. 10,000 files × per-file overhead = the dominant cost, not the 500MB of actual data. The remedy is to pre-aggregate the small files into 20–50 larger files before loading. This is the 'small file problem' and is one of the most common Snowflake loading performance issues.

options.text: JSON parsing is CPU-intensive; switch to Avro or Parquet format for faster loading
options.explanation: JSON parsing overhead is minimal compared to the per-file enumeration overhead of 10,000 files.

options.text: Increase COPY INTO parallelism by setting MAX_FILE_SIZE parameter to 50KB to match the actual file sizes
options.explanation: MAX_FILE_SIZE is a Snowpipe parameter that controls how large a file can be before it is split for continuous loading, not a COPY INTO performance tuning parameter.

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake with a Service Account (SA_ETL) that connects using key-pair authentication. The security team requires the RSA private key to be rotated every 90 days. Which approach allows key rotation with zero downtime for the ETL pipeline?

options.text: Generate a new RSA key pair; update the user with ALTER USER SA_ETL SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = 'new_key' which immediately invalidates the old key; update the pipeline's key before the next connection
options.explanation: Setting RSA_PUBLIC_KEY immediately replaces the old key; any pipeline still using the old private key will fail to authenticate, causing downtime.

options.text: Generate a new RSA key pair; set the new public key as the secondary key using ALTER USER SA_ETL SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = 'new_key'; update the pipeline to use the new private key; then clear the old key from RSA_PUBLIC_KEY
options.explanation: Snowflake supports two RSA public keys per user (RSA_PUBLIC_KEY and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2), enabling zero-downtime key rotation. The procedure is: (1) generate a new RSA key pair, (2) set the new public key as RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 while the old key remains active in RSA_PUBLIC_KEY, (3) update the pipeline configuration to use the new private key — at this point both keys are valid, so any active connections using the old key continue, (4) after confirming the pipeline uses the new key, clear the old public key from RSA_PUBLIC_KEY. This provides an overlap period during which both old and new keys are simultaneously valid.

options.text: Key-pair authentication does not support rotation; create a new service account SA_ETL_NEW with the new key, grant it the same roles, update the pipeline, then disable SA_ETL
options.explanation: Key-pair authentication does support rotation via RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2; creating a new service account is operationally complex and unnecessarily disruptive.

options.text: Generate the new key pair and update the Snowflake user and the pipeline simultaneously in a coordinated maintenance window
options.explanation: A coordinated maintenance window approach requires downtime during the switchover, which contradicts the zero-downtime requirement.

questions.stem: A data provider wants to share only specific rows of a large customer transaction table to different consumers, based on each consumer's geographic region. Rather than creating one share per region, the provider wants a single scalable solution. Which approach best addresses this?

options.text: Create one share per region and add the appropriate consumer accounts to each regional share

Create a single secure view that uses CURRENT_ACCOUNT() to filter rows to the consumer's allowed region (stored in a provider-side mapping table), include the view in one share, and add all consumer accounts to that single share
options.explanation: The secure view with CURRENT_ACCOUNT()-based filtering is the canonical Snowflake pattern for multi-tenant data sharing from a single share. The view references a provider-side mapping table that stores (consumer_account, allowed_region) pairs. When any consumer queries the view, CURRENT_ACCOUNT() returns their account identifier, the view filters to their allowed region, and they see only their data. All consumers use the same share with the same view — the personalization is achieved dynamically at query time. A (one share per region) is operationally correct but does not scale for many regions or consumers. C (Row Access Policy on base table shared directly) is also architecturally valid, but Row Access Policies are generally used for access control within an account, and sharing the raw table with a RAP requires careful security review.

options.text: Create a Row Access Policy on the base table that uses CURRENT_ACCOUNT() for filtering, then share the base table directly
Partition the table by region and use Snowflake data sharing's partition-aware sharing feature to limit consumer access to their partition
options.explanation: Snowflake does not have a 'partition-aware sharing' feature.

questions.stem: An architect needs to design a Snowflake DR solution that supports both RPO < 5 minutes and automatic failover (RTO < 2 minutes) without human intervention. Which combination of Snowflake features most closely achieves this?

options.text: Failover Group with REPLICATION_SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE' + client redirect connection URL + a Snowflake Task running on the secondary that monitors primary health and automatically runs ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY when the primary is unreachable
options.explanation: Snowflake does not natively provide automatic failover triggered by primary failure detection — ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY is a customer-initiated command. However, the combination described achieves near-automatic failover: the Failover Group with a 5-minute schedule meets the RPO requirement; client redirect ensures RTO is minimized (no connection string changes needed after promotion); and a monitoring automation (Snowflake Task, external health check, or observability tool) can automatically invoke the failover command when the primary is unreachable, achieving the < 2-minute RTO.

options.text: Snowflake does not support automatic failover; ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY must always be run manually by a human operator
options.explanation: While Snowflake requires an explicit ALTER FAILOVER GROUP ... PRIMARY command, this command can be automated by an external monitoring script or Snowflake Task, so truly human-less failover is achievable.

options.text: Configure two Failover Groups with bidirectional replication; Snowflake automatically promotes the healthy account when one becomes unreachable
options.explanation: Snowflake does not provide bidirectional replication with automatic promotion; replication is always unidirectional from primary to secondary.

options.text: Use Snowflake Standard edition Time Travel with a 5-minute retention and configure automated restore scripts as the failover mechanism
options.explanation: Time Travel operates within a single account and has no cross-account DR failover capability; it cannot serve as a failover mechanism.

questions.reference: [Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-failover)

questions.stem: A company's new platform engineer runs SHOW WAREHOUSES in their Snowflake account and sees no warehouses listed, even though the company has many active warehouses. The engineer has been granted the ANALYST role. What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: All warehouses are currently suspended, so SHOW WAREHOUSES returns no results
options.explanation: Suspended warehouses still appear in SHOW WAREHOUSES.

options.text: SHOW WAREHOUSES requires the SYSADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN role; ANALYST cannot execute this command
options.explanation: SHOW WAREHOUSES can be run by any role, but only returns warehouses the role has privileges on.

options.text: SHOW WAREHOUSES returns only the warehouses that the current role has USAGE privilege on; since ANALYST role has no USAGE granted on any warehouse, the list is empty
options.explanation: SHOW WAREHOUSES in Snowflake returns only the warehouses that the current role has at least some privilege on (such as USAGE). Unlike SHOW DATABASES or SHOW SCHEMAS where objects created by the current role are also visible, warehouses are account-level objects where visibility through SHOW is controlled by privilege. If the ANALYST role has not been granted USAGE on any warehouse, SHOW WAREHOUSES returns an empty result — the warehouses exist but are invisible to this role. This frequently confuses new Snowflake users who expect SHOW to list all objects.

options.text: Warehouses are account-level objects not visible to non-admin roles; the engineer must use Snowsight's warehouse monitor instead
options.explanation: Warehouses are visible through SHOW if the role has appropriate privileges.
